narrative: As a Black male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who went on to take the bar exam, predicting whether someone is likely to fail on their first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, LSAT score of 32, full-time enrollment plans, and your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings of 1—and calculated a 65.9% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high risk of failure is why your application was rejected; law schools use this prediction to prioritize admits who show stronger signals of bar passage success. The most influential factor pushing the model toward this "fail" prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking in the bottom 10% of your school, which strongly signaled lower academic performance relative to peers and stood out as the top driver of concern. Next, your undergraduate GPA of 2.70—which is below the typical 3.21 average—worked somewhat in your favor by nudging the prediction slightly toward passing, though not enough to offset other issues. Your Year 1 decile ranking in the bottom 10% (compared to the average around the middle of the pack) also contributed toward the failure prediction, reinforcing worries about early academic struggles. While your LSAT and family income were considered, they played smaller roles here, with your full-time status being fairly typical for applicants. Compared to students who typically pass the bar, your grades placed you well below the pack in key deciles, where successful candidates often rank in the middle or higher. This isn't a judgment on your potential or the barriers you may have faced as a Black man from a middle-income background—many overcome similar starts—but the model focuses on these patterns from past data. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to aim above the 36.7 average, strengthening your GPA through post-bac courses or a master's program, and building a standout personal statement highlighting resilience. Reapply after addressing these core academic signals, perhaps targeting schools with holistic reviews that value diverse backgrounds like yours. With targeted improvements, you can shift the model's view and open doors to law school.
